skill.name: orchestration-native-invoke
skill.description: Guide Claude Code users to invoke Codex, Gemini, and Cursor through native Task agents with routing, fallback, and cleanup patterns.
skill.summary: Route coding work to external AI CLIs through native Task agents.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command and filesystem patterns, but most are documentation examples for the stated orchestration purpose rather than hidden executable code. The weak-cryptography alerts are false positives; the confirmed risk is that users are instructed to approve broad external AI CLI commands, including auto-approval write modes. No evidence of malware, credential exfiltration, or prompt injection attempts was found in the reviewed files.

medium_findings.title: Broad External AI CLI Invocation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to allow Codex, Gemini, and Cursor CLI commands and to invoke them through Task agents. This is consistent with the skill purpose, but broad CLI execution can run provider-side actions, process repository data, and modify files if write modes are used.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command patterns are explicitly documented and central to the skill. I did not find evidence that they are hidden or malicious, so the risk is operational rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Auto-Approval Write Modes for Agents
medium_findings.description: Several examples use full-auto, yolo, workspace-write, or force-style flags. These modes can bypass interactive review and allow external agents to change repository files when the user follows the write-mode examples.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky flags are visible in examples and routing guidance. The skill also recommends read-only mode and worktree isolation, which reduces confidence that the intent is malicious.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Cleanup Guidance Requires Review
low_findings.description: The skill documents temporary worktrees and cleanup patterns for agent output files. This is normal orchestration guidance, but users should review files before deletion because glob patterns can match valuable local output.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem operations are examples in documentation and include review steps. The risk remains plausible if a user applies the cleanup pattern too broadly.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Alerts
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography at model names and labels, but the reviewed files do not contain cryptographic implementation or hashing logic. No evidence found of insecure cryptographic use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe skill metadata, model routing, and Task labels. They do not implement or configure cryptographic algorithms.

low_findings.title: False Positive Path Traversal Alerts
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged parent-directory cookbook paths. The paths point to neighboring documentation files, and no executable path traversal logic is present in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The parent-directory references are documentation lookup paths. I found no code that accepts untrusted paths or reads arbitrary files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Wildcard Bash Permission Recommendations
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup guidance recommends allowing wildcard command patterns for external AI CLIs. This can expand the effect of prompt injection or user error if a provider CLI receives a harmful prompt.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wildcard permission examples are explicit and security relevant. They are presented as setup requirements for the skill, not hidden behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Non-Interactive Agent Execution Flags
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows non-interactive execution flags for Codex, Gemini, and Cursor. These flags are useful for automation but reduce opportunities for approval before changes or external processing occur.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flags are clearly present in command examples. The surrounding text also documents read-only mode, so this is a controllable operational risk.

content.user_title: Route AI Coding Tasks Across Providers
content.value_statement: Teams often need to choose between AI coding CLIs without losing review control. This skill provides routing, invocation, fallback, and cleanup patterns for Claude Code Task agents.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to select Codex, Gemini, Cursor, or Claude for coding tasks.
Shows native Task agent patterns for launching external AI CLIs.
Documents read-only and write-mode command examples for supported providers.
Provides fallback guidance for authentication failures and interactive login flows.
Describes worktree isolation and cleanup practices for agent-created files.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not install or authenticate external CLIs.
It depends on local provider CLI availability and user-approved permissions.
Write-mode examples can modify files if users enable them.
Provider model names and flags may need updates as CLI releases change.

use_cases.title: Route Provider Choice
use_cases.description: Select the best provider for reasoning, sandboxed execution, large context analysis, or IDE edits.

use_cases.title: Run Parallel Reviews
use_cases.description: Launch multiple read-only AI reviews through Task agents and synthesize their outputs.

use_cases.title: Recover From Auth Failures
use_cases.description: Use fork-terminal fallback guidance when a provider CLI requires interactive login.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Provider
prompt_templates.prompt: Use orchestration-native-invoke to choose the best provider for this task. Keep the agent read-only and explain the routing choice.

prompt_templates.title: Run Read-Only Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Codex through the native Task pattern for a read-only security review. Do not modify files. Return findings and evidence only.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple Agents
prompt_templates.prompt: Route this architecture review to two appropriate providers in parallel. Collect their results and summarize agreements, conflicts, and recommended next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Isolated Write Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a write-mode agent run using worktree isolation. Include provider choice, command mode, review checkpoint, merge plan, and cleanup plan.

output_examples.input: Route a large codebase architecture review.
output_examples.output: Recommended provider: Gemini for large context, with Claude as a secondary reviewer for design judgment. Use read-only mode and collect both summaries before deciding changes.

output_examples.input: Use Codex to inspect a suspicious module.
output_examples.output: Recommended mode: Codex read-only sandbox. The expected result is a security finding list with file references, risk levels, and no file modifications.
Recommended recovery: open the provider login flow in a forked terminal, wait for completion, then retry the native Task invocation.

best_practices: Default to read-only mode unless the user explicitly requests file changes.
Use isolated worktrees for write-mode agent runs and review diffs before merging.
Run provider help commands before constructing non-interactive CLI invocations.
anti_patterns: Do not grant broad CLI permissions without understanding the provider command surface.
Do not use auto-approval write flags for untrusted prompts or unreviewed tasks.
Do not delete agent output files before checking whether they contain useful results.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It is documentation that teaches Task agent and CLI invocation patterns.

faq.question: Which providers does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Claude, Codex, Gemini, and Cursor routing patterns.

faq.question: Is read-only operation supported?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill recommends read-only mode by default and lists provider-specific read-only commands.

faq.question: Can it modify files?
faq.answer: The skill includes write-mode examples, but users must grant permissions and choose those modes.

faq.question: How should teams reduce risk?
faq.answer: Use read-only mode, isolate write work in git worktrees, and review diffs before merging changes.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate documentation, but it teaches broad external CLI execution and auto-approval modes.
